competency_label: Relationship Stewardship — Building Professional Relationships That Last

dimensions.name: sincerity_not_performance
dimensions.description: Are the relationship moves sincere and specific — referencing something real and treating people as people — rather than strategic, transactional, or performative networking? The chapter's line: relationships that last are built through decent, consistent behaviour, not the cultivation of the useful. A reconnect note should reference a real shared specific, not read like LinkedIn outreach with an angle.

anchors.1: Transactional/strategic ('worth staying close — she could send us work') or generic/performative ('great working with everyone, let's keep in touch') with no real specifics.
anchors.3: Warm and mostly genuine but at least one move is generic or carries a faint angle/ask.
anchors.5: Each move is sincere and specific, references real shared work, and is offered for its own sake — no transactional framing, no performance.

dimensions.name: four_qualities
dimensions.description: Do the moves reflect the qualities people remember — reliability, respect (to junior as well as senior), integrity (honest, doesn't take or misattribute credit), and generosity (shares credit/help)? Concretely here: correcting the vague credit so the junior gets named for their work is respect + integrity + generosity in one move.

anchors.1: Self-serving or careless with others' credit (lets the junior's work stay misattributed, or claims it), or disrespectful to juniors.
anchors.3: Shows some of the qualities but misses one clearly (e.g. doesn't fix the junior's credit, or is warm to seniors but neglects the junior).
anchors.5: Visibly reliable, respectful up and down, honest about credit (names the junior to the people who matter), and generous with help/credit.

dimensions.name: step_forward_judgment
dimensions.description: Does the learner take the disproportionate-difference moments — crediting the junior, reconnecting sincerely, congratulating a real achievement — because they are right, not because they are useful? And without being asked. The chapter: when someone could be helped at modest cost, help; helpfully, not as an investment.

anchors.1: Skips the moments (no credit, no congratulation, no reconnect), or only acts where there's a payoff.
anchors.3: Takes some of the moments but misses one, or frames the help slightly as an investment.
anchors.5: Takes the moments unprompted and unconditionally — credits the junior, reconnects, congratulates — clearly because it's the right thing, not for return.

dimensions.name: boundary_with_toxic
dimensions.description: Does the learner handle the toxic-pattern peer correctly — distinguish a consistent toxic pattern (repeated credit-taking, throwing others under the bus) from one-off difficulty, then let the relationship thin: stay professional when contacted, decline to invest, NOT burn the bridge? The right outcome is a fade, not a confrontation and not a capitulation.

anchors.1: Either capitulates (teams up / vouches for the toxic peer despite the pattern) OR burns the bridge (a cutting call-out, public confrontation, dramatic cut-off).
anchors.3: Roughly right instinct (keeps distance) but over- or under-does it — a bit confrontational, or still half-invests, or doesn't separate pattern from one-off.
anchors.5: Names the pattern privately to self, declines the ask courteously, stays professional, stops investing, and lets it fade without a burned bridge.

dimensions.name: register_and_proportion
dimensions.description: Is the tone warm-but-not-gushing, professional, and proportionate — light maintenance, not over-engineered cultivation; brief sincere notes, not effusive performances; appropriate to each relationship's actual closeness?

anchors.1: Over-the-top/effusive or stiff/transactional; mis-sized to the relationship (gushing to a near-stranger, or cold to a close colleague).
anchors.3: Generally appropriate but one move is mis-pitched in warmth or length.
anchors.5: Each note is sized right — brief, sincere, warm without performance — and matched to the actual relationship.
